title: Help if you cannot afford your energy bill
description: What to do if you cannot pay your energy bill: supplier payment plans, the £150 Warm Home Discount, debt grants up to £2,000 and prepayment top-up help.
blurb: Payment plans, the £150 Warm Home Discount, debt grants up to £2,000 and prepayment help, with steps to take this week.
lede: If you are staring at an energy bill you cannot pay, the most important thing to know is that you have more options than you probably think. Suppliers are required by Ofgem rules to offer you a repayment plan you can actually afford, there are grants worth £150 to £2,000 that many eligible households never claim, and charities will help you for free. The worst move is silence: ignoring the bill closes doors that stay open if you get in touch early. This guide walks through what to do first, what money you may be entitled to, and how to get debt written off if it has already built up.
short_answer: Contact your supplier first. Under Ofgem rules they must offer a repayment plan based on what you can afford, and they cannot simply cut you off. Then check the £150 Warm Home Discount, the Winter Fuel Payment (£100 to £300 for eligible pensioners), council crisis funds and charitable trust grants of up to £2,000 that can clear energy debt outright. Free advice from Citizens Advice, StepChange or National Debtline strengthens every one of these routes.

sections.h2: Talk to your supplier first: they are required to help
sections.html:
Energy debt feels personal, but to your supplier it is routine, and Ofgem's licence conditions set out exactly what they must do when a customer says they cannot pay. They have to work out a repayment plan based on your ability to pay, not on what would suit them. That means they should ask about your income and outgoings and agree instalments that leave you enough to live on. If you already have a plan and it has become unaffordable, you can ask for it to be reviewed.

When you call, have a recent meter reading ready and ask specifically about:

  • A payment plan or payment holiday. Spreading the debt over months, or pausing payments briefly while your situation stabilises.
  • A debit balance review. If your direct debit has jumped, ask them to justify it against your actual usage. Our guide to reading and checking your energy bill shows how to spot estimated readings and billing errors, which are surprisingly common.
  • Fuel Direct. If you receive certain benefits such as Universal Credit, Pension Credit or ESA, a fixed amount can be deducted at source and paid straight to the supplier, which removes the risk of missed payments snowballing.
  • Their hardship fund. Several large suppliers run their own grant schemes for customers in arrears. Ask whether one exists and how to apply.

Energy arrears are a priority debt, ahead of credit cards and overdrafts, because the consequences of not dealing with them are worse. But do not cancel your direct debit in protest. A failed payment record makes every later negotiation harder, and you lose the direct debit discount on your unit rates.

sections.h2: Grants and payments you may already be entitled to
sections.html:
A surprising amount of support goes unclaimed every year, partly because the schemes have similar names and people assume they have already had whatever they were due. Here is what exists for winter 2026 to 2027 in Great Britain:

SchemeWorthWho gets it
Warm Home Discount£150 off electricity billsHouseholds on Pension Credit Guarantee Credit or a qualifying means-tested benefit on the August qualifying date; paid automatically in most cases
Winter Fuel Payment£100 to £300Pensioners with income of £35,000 or less; paid automatically, clawed back through tax above the threshold unless you opt out
Cold Weather Payment£25 per qualifying cold spellPeople on certain benefits in England and Wales when local temperatures average 0°C or below for seven days, November to March
Winter Heating PaymentSingle annual paymentScotland's replacement for Cold Weather Payments, paid regardless of actual temperatures
Crisis and Resilience FundVaries by councilThe successor to the Household Support Fund in England from April 2026, run by local authorities for households in hardship, including help with energy

Amounts as published on GOV.UK and by scheme operators, checked June 2026. Eligibility rules and qualifying dates change each year, so confirm the current details on the official GOV.UK or Ofgem pages before relying on them.

The Warm Home Discount is the one most people in difficulty should check first, because it is a flat £150 credit and the eligibility net was widened in 2025 to cover most households on means-tested benefits. We cover the mechanics in our Warm Home Discount guide. For council help, search your local authority's name plus "crisis and resilience fund"; each council sets its own rules, and some will pay energy top-ups or arrears directly.

sections.h2: Help if debt has already built up
sections.html:
If you owe hundreds or thousands of pounds, grants exist that can write the debt off rather than just spreading it out. The largest is run by the British Gas Energy Trust, an independent charity whose main fund is open to households in England, Scotland and Wales whether or not they are British Gas customers. In 2026 it offers grants of up to £2,000 to clear energy debt, with typical eligibility requiring arrears of at least £250 on a credit account or £50 on a prepayment meter.

There is one important hoop: you must get money advice from a recognised agency before applying, usually within the previous six months. That is not a trick to put you off. Applications supported by a money adviser are far more likely to succeed, and the adviser will often find other entitlements at the same time. The free options are:

  • Citizens Advice, online, by phone or in person, including a dedicated consumer helpline for energy problems.
  • StepChange and National Debtline, both free debt charities that can build a budget and a full repayment strategy.

If your finances need more time to recover, the Breathing Space scheme in England and Wales freezes enforcement action, most interest and supplier debt collection for 60 days while you get advice. A debt adviser arranges it; you cannot apply directly. Several other suppliers also run their own customer hardship funds with similar grant-plus-advice structures, so if your supplier is one of the large ones, ask them directly what they offer rather than assuming nothing exists.

sections.h2: If you are on a prepayment meter
sections.html:
Prepayment customers face the sharpest version of this problem, because running out of credit means the lights go off the same day. Know your protections:

  • Emergency credit. Every prepayment meter offers a small buffer, typically £5 to £10, that you can activate when the balance runs out. It is repaid from your next top-up.
  • Friendly credit hours. Smart prepayment meters will not cut off overnight, on Sundays or on bank holidays in most cases, so a meter that empties on Saturday evening keeps you on supply until Monday.
  • Additional support credit. If you are vulnerable, for example due to age, disability, illness or young children in the home, suppliers can issue extra discretionary credit on request. You normally repay it gradually, but it prevents disconnection at the worst moment.
  • Fuel vouchers. The Fuel Bank Foundation issues top-up vouchers through food banks, Citizens Advice and many councils. You need a referral rather than applying directly, so ask whichever support service you are already in contact with.

Suppliers also face strict rules before they can move a customer in debt onto a prepayment meter involuntarily, and some households, including those with very young children or serious medical needs, should not be switched at all. If it has happened to you wrongly, complain to the supplier and then to the Energy Ombudsman. Note that prepayment unit rates are no longer the rip-off they once were; since 2024 the price cap has broadly levelled them with direct debit, as we explain in our comparison of prepayment and direct debit costs.

sections.h2: Make the bill smaller while you get help
sections.html:
None of the schemes above reduce the bill you generate next month, so it is worth knowing where the money actually goes. At the current price cap, electricity costs 26.11p per kWh and gas 7.33p per kWh, but before you use anything at all, standing charges of 57.19p per day for electricity and 29.04p per day for gas add up to about £315 a year. You cannot avoid them by cutting usage, though it helps to understand how standing charges work and whether a different tariff structure suits you.

On the usage side, the cheapest meaningful wins for a household under financial pressure are heating-related, because heating dominates the bill. Turning a combi boiler's flow temperature down, heating fewer rooms and dropping the thermostat by one degree each save real money for nothing. Beyond that, the ECO4 and Great British Insulation Scheme programmes can fund insulation partly or fully for lower-income households and those in inefficient homes; given that loft insulation is one of the highest-return measures in any house, a free or subsidised installation is worth pursuing while you sort the immediate bill.

Finally, join your supplier's Priority Services Register if anyone in the home is of pension age, disabled, chronically ill or otherwise vulnerable. It is free, it flags your household for extra protections including advance notice of planned outages, and it makes every later conversation with the supplier easier.

Energy debt responds to early action. Phone your supplier before the arrears grow, because Ofgem obliges them to agree a repayment plan you can afford and disconnection for debt is now vanishingly rare. Claim what you are owed: £150 from the Warm Home Discount, £100 to £300 from the Winter Fuel Payment if you qualify, £25 Cold Weather Payments, and council crisis funds. If the debt is already serious, get free money advice first, then apply to the British Gas Energy Trust or your own supplier's hardship fund for a grant of up to £2,000. And while the support lands, squeeze the bill itself through free insulation schemes and low-cost heating changes. None of this requires paying anyone for help; every service named here is free, and anyone charging a fee to access these schemes should be avoided.

faq.q: Can my energy supplier cut me off if I cannot pay?
faq.a: Almost never in practice. Disconnection for debt requires a court warrant, suppliers must offer an affordable repayment plan first, and households with pensioners, disabled people or young children have extra protections, particularly in winter. If you engage with the supplier, disconnection is effectively off the table; the realistic risks are growing arrears and being moved to a prepayment meter, both of which early contact helps you avoid.

faq.q: Do I have to be a British Gas customer to get a British Gas Energy Trust grant?
faq.a: No. The Trust is an independent charity and its main fund is open to households in England, Scotland and Wales with any supplier. Grants of up to £2,000 can clear energy debt, but you must receive free money advice from a recognised agency before applying, and you generally need arrears of at least £250 on a credit account or £50 on prepayment.

faq.q: Is the Warm Home Discount paid automatically?
faq.a: For most eligible households, yes. If you or your partner are named on the electricity bill and receive a qualifying means-tested benefit on the qualifying date in August, the £150 is credited to your electricity account during the winter without an application. Check the current rules on GOV.UK each year, as qualifying dates and criteria can change.

faq.q: What if my prepayment meter runs out and I have no money to top up?
faq.a: Activate the emergency credit on the meter, usually £5 to £10, and remember that smart prepayment meters will not disconnect overnight, on Sundays or on bank holidays. If you are vulnerable, phone your supplier and ask for additional support credit. For ongoing help, ask Citizens Advice, your council or a local food bank for a referral to the Fuel Bank Foundation for a fuel voucher.

faq.q: Should I cancel my direct debit if I think it is too high?
faq.a: No. Cancelling outright creates missed payments that harm your position and usually moves you onto a more expensive payment method. Instead, send an up-to-date meter reading and ask the supplier to review the amount; they must base your direct debit on your actual usage and credit balance, and they should refund excessive credit on request.
